title: Congenital Heart Disease
authors: Natasha González Estévez & Deidra A. Ansah

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Congenital Heart Disease?
CHD: ~1% of live births — most common birth defect; HLHS alone = 23% cardiac deaths in first week of life. Ductal-dependent lesions: ductus closes (day 1-3) → cardiovascular collapse → start PGE1 IMMEDIATELY. Pulse oximetry screening detects 'critical CHD' before symptoms — universal in term newborns. 'Well baby, then suddenly collapsed on day 2-3' = ductal-dependent CHD until proven otherwise. Prenatal diagnosis + tertiary center delivery → ↓morbidity; surgical outcomes continue to improve dramatically.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย fetal-to-postnatal transition: PVR drop, ductal/foramen ovale closure, ↑LV dominance
รู้ pulse oximetry screening: target, indication, which lesions detected/missed
รู้ 4 etiologic categories of CHD with clinical examples
แยก left-to-right shunt lesions: PDA, VSD, ASD, AVSD — symptoms, timing, management
แยก cyanotic CHD: TGA, TOF, Tricuspid atresia, Ebstein, Truncus arteriosus, TAPVR — presentation + initial management
รู้ ductus-dependent systemic circulation: HLHS, critical coarctation, IAA, critical AS → PGE1
รู้ ductus-dependent pulmonary circulation: PA/IVS, TOF with severe RVOT obstruction → PGE1
รู้ HLHS: physiology (parallel circulation), preoperative management (Qp:Qs balancing), Norwood/Sano/Hybrid procedures
รู้ surgical strategies: arterial switch (TGA), TOF repair, Norwood (HLHS), Fontan pathway
รู้ genetics: 22q11 (TBX1, conotruncal), trisomy 21 (AVSD), Turner (coarctation, bicuspid AV)
1. Fetal-to-Postnatal Transition
1.1 Fetal Circulation
Low SVR (placenta); high PVR; circulations in PARALLEL
Ductus venosus: umbilical vein → IVC bypassing liver (oxygenated blood to heart)
Foramen ovale: oxygenated placental blood → left heart → brain + coronary arteries (priority organs)
Ductus arteriosus: RV output → descending aorta + placenta (bypasses pulmonary vasculature)
RV: dominant fetal ventricle — carries primary workload; handles ~65% combined cardiac output
1.2 Transition at Birth
First breath → ↑O₂ → ↑NO → ↓PVR → >8× ↑pulmonary blood flow
Cord clamping → removes low-resistance placenta from circulation → ↑SVR
Foramen ovale: ↑LA pressure → flap closes (PFO may persist in 20-30% adults)
Ductus arteriosus: ↑O₂ + ↓PGE₂ → functional closure hours 1-12; anatomic closure over days to weeks
RV remodels (thinner, more compliant) over 6-8 weeks; LV becomes dominant
PVR continues to fall over 6-8 weeks postnatally (postnatal pulmonary vascular remodeling)
1.3 Implications for CHD
↓PVR over weeks: left-to-right shunts worsen over time → VSD/PDA symptoms often not apparent until weeks 2-6
Ductal-dependent lesions: symptoms begin when PDA closes (typically day 1-7); may initially appear 'normal well baby'
Tachypnea without tachycardia: classic presenting symptom of CHF in neonate (distinguish from sepsis: fever, CRP)
2. Clinical Evaluation and Screening
2.1 Newborn Physical Examination
Four extremity BPs: right arm (pre-ductal) + leg (post-ductal); right arm > left leg by >10 mmHg → coarctation
Pre-ductal + post-ductal SpO₂ simultaneously: right hand + right foot
Pre > post: R→L ductal shunt — PPHN, coarctation, severe HLHS
Post > pre (reversed differential cyanosis): → TGA + PPHN; post-ductal blood from PA (via PDA) is oxygenated from pulmonary circuit
Second heart sound: physiologic split = virtually always structurally normal heart; single loud S2 = pulmonary or aortic valve abnormality
Continuous murmur over head/liver → AVM → high-output failure
2.2 Pulse Oximetry Screening
Target: detect 'critical CHD' — requires intervention in first month of life
Protocol: SpO₂ in right hand (pre-ductal) + foot (post-ductal); performed after 24 hr of age
Screen POSITIVE if: SpO₂ <90% either site; OR SpO₂ <95% both sites on 3 separate measures; OR difference right hand vs foot >3%
Lesions well-detected: TGA, PA/IVS, critical PS, HLHS, truncus, TAPVR, coarctation (differential cyanosis)
Lesions MISSED: coarctation without cyanosis, large VSD, AVSD (normal SpO₂) → still detected by clinical exam + femoral pulses
2.3 Oxygen Challenge Test
Inspired O₂ → 100% × 5 min → if SpO₂ rises to normal → suggests lung disease (V/Q mismatch)
Cyanotic structural CHD: limited improvement with 100% O₂ (fixed R→L intracardiac shunt
Cannot distinguish 100% accurately — history + exam + echo essential; hyperoxia test supports but does not replace imaging
2.4 ECG Age-Dependent Standards
Normal neonate ECG: prominent rightward forces; upright T in right precordial leads (first few days of life)
Extreme right axis deviation + Q waves in I, aVL → complete AVSD
Preexcitation + RA enlargement → Ebstein anomaly (WPW common with Ebstein)
Left axis deviation + LVH → tricuspid atresia
Q waves V1-V3 → congenitally corrected TGA
3. Genetics and CHD
3.1 Overview
~30% CHD associated with chromosomal abnormality; ~400 genes associated with CHD; ~2% point mutations; 10-15% copy number variants
Genetic testing algorithm: suspect aneuploidy → karyotype or FISH; suspect 22q11 → FISH; severe CHD + extracardiac → chromosomal microarray; single-gene syndrome → whole exome sequencing
3.2 Key Genetic Associations

rows: IAA type B, TOF, truncus arteriosus, conotruncal defects; hypocalcemia, thymic aplasia, cognitive delay; irradiated blood products mandatory
Complete AVSD (most common), VSD, ASD; ↑risk PPHN; slower PVR drop postnatally
VSD, ASD, complex defects; poor prognosis
VSD, ASD, complex defects; poor prognosis
Bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation, HLHS variant; aortic surveillance lifelong
Pulmonary stenosis (most common), HCM, ASD; normal chromosomes
Supravalvar AS, branch PA stenosis; elfin facies, hypercalcemia
ASD, VSD, heart block; upper limb abnormalities

blocks.text: 4. Left-to-Right Shunt Lesions
4.1 Patent Ductus Arteriosus (Term Infant)
Full-term PDA: more common in females; genetic component (chromosome 12); distinct from PDA of prematurity
Pathophysiology: aorta → pulmonary artery shunt; magnitude depends on ductus diameter + PVR/SVR ratio
Small PDA: asymptomatic; 'machinery murmur' (continuous) at left infraclavicular region; wide pulse pressure
Large PDA: CHF as PVR drops; bounding pulses; diastolic run-off from aorta
Management: catheter closure preferred in non-premature infants >4 kg; device closure (Piccolo and others) in cath lab
4.2 Ventricular Septal Defect (VSD) — Most Common CHD

rows: Component of AVSD; rarely spontaneous closure
Progressive AI from right coronary cusp prolapse

blocks.text: Timing: small VSD → loud murmur day 1-2; large VSD → symptoms weeks 2-6 as PVR drops (NOT loud at birth — must educate parents)
Management: medical (diuretics ± ACE inhibitor); surgery if: (1) failure to thrive; (2) CHF uncontrolled; (3) Qp:Qs >2.0; (4) outlet VSD; (5) ↑PVR; always rule out coarctation!
4.3 Atrial Septal Defect (ASD)
Types: secundum (most common), primum (part of AVSD spectrum), sinus venosus (associated anomalous pulmonary venous drainage), coronary sinus
Neonatal ASD: usually bidirectional; no murmur in neonatal period (RV still stiff → limits L→R shunt)
Classic (older child): fixed split S2; systolic ejection murmur (upper left sternal border); diastolic inflow rumble (↑tricuspid flow)
Treatment: typically delayed to 3-4 years; device closure in cath lab (>15 kg, adequate rim); surgery if large or deficient aortic rim
4.4 Atrioventricular Septal Defect (AVSD)
Complete AVSD: common AV valve + primum ASD + inlet VSD → most common CHD in Down syndrome (trisomy 21)
Pathophysiology: AV-level + ventricular-level L→R shunting; common AV valve regurgitation ↑volume load
ECG: superior axis (-30 to -120°) + RVH + Q waves in I and aVL → virtually diagnostic
Critical assessment: ventricular balance (biventricular repair?); degree of AV valve regurgitation
Management: CHF control (diuretics, ACE inhibitor); surgical repair at 4-6 months; EARLY repair if no ↓PVR (↑risk irreversible PVD, especially non-Down syndrome)
Down syndrome: PVR ↓ slower than non-Down → some natural protection; avoid ↑O₂ (worsens CHF by ↓PVR → ↑L→R shunt → more respiratory distress)
5. Cyanotic Congenital Heart Disease
5.1 Transposition of the Great Arteries (D-TGA)
5% of all CHD; most common cyanotic CHD presenting in newborn period; 60-70% male
Anatomy: aorta from RV (anterior + rightward) + PA from LV → parallel circulations; survival requires mixing
Clinical: cyanosis within hours, NOT responsive to O₂; loud single S2; CXR 'egg on string' (narrow mediastinum, ↑pulmonary vascularity)
Mixing: bidirectional PFO + PDA; 40-45% associated VSD (improves mixing but complicates surgery)
Initial management: PGE₁ (0.03-0.1 mcg/kg/min) to maintain DA; BAS (balloon atrial septostomy) if restrictive PFO + persistent hypoxia + acidosis despite PDA
Surgery: arterial switch operation (Jatene) ideally within first WEEK of life (before LV depressurizes); coronary artery transfer included; outcomes excellent >95% survival
5.2 Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF)
Most common cyanotic CHD overall (10% all CHD); 25% right aortic arch
4 components: VSD + overriding aorta + RVOT obstruction + RVH — all from anterior deviation infundibular septum
22q11 deletion in 20%; TOF pulmonary atresia = extreme form
Clinical spectrum by RVOT obstruction:
Mild RVOT (Pink Tet): predominant L→R shunt; CHF symptoms; normal PAs; may not have cyanosis at rest
Moderate RVOT: bidirectional shunt; mild cyanosis; Tet spells (hypercyanotic episodes from agitation → acute ↑RVOT spasm → ↑R→L shunting)
Severe RVOT/PA: ductal-dependent pulmonary circulation → PGE₁ immediately; severely cyanotic at birth
Tet spell treatment: knee-chest position + O₂ + morphine (↓agitation, ↓hyperpnea) + IV fluids + phenylephrine (↑SVR) + propranolol (↓RVOT spasm); recurrent → surgical urgency
Surgery: complete repair (VSD closure + RVOT augmentation) preferred 4-6 months; symptomatic neonates → modified BT shunt OR primary complete repair (trend toward earlier repair)
TOF with absent pulmonary valve: 'to and fro' murmur; massively dilated PAs → airway compression → respiratory failure; surgical urgency in severe cases
5.3 Pulmonary Atresia with Intact Ventricular Septum (PA/IVS)
PA leaflets fused/absent → RV cannot empty → systemic venous return via PFO to left heart
Pulmonary blood flow: entirely ductal-dependent → PGE₁ essential
RV-dependent coronary circulation: RV sinusoids → retrograde coronary perfusion; decompressing RV → ↓coronary perfusion → myocardial ischemia/infarction → cardiac cath MANDATORY before RVOT opening

Management: (1) No RV-dependent coronary → RVOT augmentation (surgical or catheter perforation + valvuloplasty); (2) RV-dependent coronary → systemic-PA shunt only → staged single ventricle palliation ± transplant
ECG: leftward QRS axis, absent right forces, peaked P waves (RA enlargement)
5.4 Tricuspid Atresia
No outlet from RA to RV; systemic venous return → PFO/ASD → LA → LV
Associated VSD in >90%; determines pulmonary blood flow (small VSD → ductal-dependent)
Clinical: cyanosis; holosystolic murmur; ECG virtually diagnostic: left axis deviation + LVH (LV does ALL work)
Management: PGE₁ if ductal-dependent; Fontan pathway: BT shunt neonatal → bidirectional Glenn ~6 months → Fontan ~3 years
Variant with TGA: aortic blood flow may be restricted by VSD → coarctation-like obstruction possible
5.5 Ebstein Anomaly
Downward displacement of septal + posterior tricuspid leaflets; 'atrialized' RV; severe TR; large RA
Associated with WPW syndrome → arrhythmia risk (AVRT, AF, sudden death)
Spectrum: mild → symptoms later in life; severe: cyanosis at birth, massive cardiomegaly (CXR 'wall-to-wall heart'), functional pulmonary atresia
Neonatal severe Ebstein: PGE₁ to maintain ductal patency; O₂ + iNO + mild alkalosis → ↓PVR → improved RV contribution to PA flow → wean PGE₁ over days
If PVR management fails → surgical: TV repair/replacement + RV exclusion ± BT shunt; perinatal mortality up to 45% for severe cases
5.6 Truncus Arteriosus
Single arterial trunk → systemic + pulmonary + coronary circulation; overrides VSD; 22q11 in ~50%
Classification: Type I (short main PA from trunk) / Type II (separate bilateral PA from posterior trunk) / Type III (widely separate PA origins)
Pathophysiology: as PVR ↓ → ↑pulmonary overcirculation → CHF; rarely cyanotic initially (mixed blood to all beds) → CHF develops weeks 2-6
Clinical: minimal cyanosis initially; CHF weeks 2-6; loud single S2; wide pulse pressure (run-off to lungs)
Management: CHF control medically; surgical repair within first 2 months (VSD closure + RV-PA conduit); conduit requires multiple replacements over lifetime
5.7 Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Return (TAPVR)
Types: Supracardiac (most common; vertical vein → left innominate → SVC → RA), Cardiac (coronary sinus), Infracardiac (descending vein → portal/hepatic vein), Mixed
Unobstructed: minimal symptoms neonatal period; right heart failure develops over weeks; soft systolic murmur; fixed split S2; surgery less urgent
Obstructed (surgical emergency!): infracardiac most common; pulmonary venous HTN → pulmonary edema → profound cyanosis + respiratory distress NOT responsive to O₂
CXR obstructed TAPVR: normal heart size + pulmonary venous congestion ('interstitial pneumonia' pattern); echo essential (small pulmonary veins to LA)
Surgery URGENTLY required for obstructed TAPVR; PGE₁ does NOT significantly help (obstruction not ductal-dependent); postoperatively: PVR lability + risk late pulmonary vein re-stenosis (~20%)
6. Ductal-Dependent Lesions and Management Principles

rows: PDA allows mixing; PFO too restrictive alone
PGE₁ → BT shunt or complete repair
Complete PA obstruction; RV cannot pump to lungs
PGE₁ → RVOT opening (if no RV-dep coronary)
VSD too restrictive → insufficient PA flow
RV provides ALL systemic output via DA
TR + elevated RA pressure → R→L PFO; functional PA
PGE₁ → RV function recovery or surgical

blocks.text:
PGE₁ Dosing Protocol
Starting dose: 0.03-0.05 mcg/kg/min IV; may ↑ to 0.1 mcg/kg/min if no response; always use LOWEST effective dose. Most common side effect: apnea → may require intubation (prepare before starting PGE₁, especially in transport). Other effects: fever, hypotension, jitteriness, cortical hyperostosis (chronic). Keep warm. Monitor BP + SpO₂ + peripheral perfusion continuously.
7. HLHS — Focus Section
7.1 Anatomy and Physiology
Aortic + mitral atresia/hypoplasia + hypoplastic LV + hypoplastic aortic arch — LV cannot support systemic circulation
Parallel circulation: both systemic AND pulmonary blood flow from RV via PDA; Qp:Qs determined by relative PVR/SVR
As PVR ↓ postnatally: ↑pulmonary blood flow at expense of systemic flow → ↑SpO₂ + metabolic acidosis + low BP — paradoxical!
Restrictive PFO: pulmonary venous blood cannot egress LA → pulmonary venous HTN → pulmonary edema; presents with profound cyanosis + respiratory distress at birth (surgical/interventional emergency)
7.2 Preoperative Management
Target SpO₂: 75-85% = adequate Qp:Qs ~1:1; mixed venous O₂ sat target ~25 points below systemic (SVO₂ ~50%)
↑PVR tools (↓Qp): (1) Avoid supplemental O₂; (2) FiO₂ 0.17 (N₂ or CO₂ added to circuit); CO₂ more effective (↑systemic DO₂); target FiO₂ 0.17-0.21; (3) ↑PEEP; (4) Permissive hypercapnia
Systemic hemodynamics: milrinone (afterload reduction + modest inotropy) ± dopamine; vasopressin if refractory hypotension
Restrictive PFO: BAS or surgical septectomy urgently before Norwood (life-threatening pulmonary venous obstruction)
Elective intubation: controversial; many prefer spontaneous ventilation while manipulating inspired gases to avoid ↑infection + labile hemodynamics
7.3 Surgical Palliation Options

rows: Aortic arch reconstruction + systemic-PA shunt for stable pulmonary flow
Same arch reconstruction + RV-PA conduit (pulsatile flow, no diastolic run-off/coronary steal)
Ductal stent + bilateral PA banding; avoids CPB in neonatal period
SVC → PA; unloads RV; reduces volume load
Complete separation pulmonary + systemic circulations; passive pulmonary flow

blocks.text: Survival: stage I neonatal mortality ~10-20% (improving significantly); 10-year survival 74% (heart transplant) or comparable with surgical palliation at experienced centers
8. NICU Practical — CHD Approach
'Day 2-3 collapse' formula: ductal-dependent lesion → start PGE₁ 0.05 mcg/kg/min BEFORE full workup if unstable + cyanotic + not improving with O₂
Systematic initial assessment: (1) Four extremity BPs; (2) Pre/post-ductal SpO₂; (3) O₂ challenge test; (4) 12-lead ECG; (5) CXR; (6) Bedside echo + immediate cardiology consult
Cyanosis NOT responsive to O₂: cyanotic CHD until proven otherwise; give PGE₁ if unstable
Tachypnea + normal SpO₂ + no fever: left-to-right shunt CHF (VSD, AVSD, PDA) as PVR drops; start diuretics; outpatient follow-up
VSD + no symptoms at birth → symptoms at 2-6 wk → normal evolution; CXR to track pulmonary vascular markings; reassure parents
AVSD in Down syndrome: caution with supplemental O₂ (↓PVR → ↑L→R → worsen CHF)
Restrictive PFO in TGA: BAS urgently (interventional cardiology); stable after BAS; arterial switch within first week
Pre-surgery HLHS: aim SpO₂ 75-85%; NO supplemental O₂; monitor lactate + mixed venous sat; milrinone ± vasopressin; deliver at tertiary center if prenatally diagnosed
9. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — 'Day 2-3 Well → Collapsed' = Ductal-Dependent Lesion
PGE₁ ทันที: 0.05 mcg/kg/min; ductus ปิดตอนวันที่ 1-3 ใน term infants; lesions: HLHS, critical coarctation, IAA, critical AS, severe TOF/PA, PA/IVS. BEFORE echo results: ถ้า unstable + cyanotic + not improving O₂ → เริ่ม PGE₁. Most common side effect: apnea → prepare intubation. CXR: ถ้า small heart + pulmonary edema → obstructed TAPVR (PGE₁ does NOT help → surgical emergency).

PEARL 2 — Reversed Differential Cyanosis = TGA + PPHN
Normal differential cyanosis: pre-ductal (right hand) SpO₂ > post-ductal (foot). Reversed: foot SpO₂ > right hand SpO₂ → occurs in TGA with PPHN + PDA: PA blood (oxygenated via lungs) → DA → descending aorta (post-ductal) + desaturated RV blood → aorta (pre-ductal). เจอ reversed differential cyanosis → think TGA + PPHN immediately; management: PGE₁ + consider iNO + echo ด่วน + BAS ถ้า restrictive PFO.

PEARL 3 — Pulse Oximetry Screening: Normal SpO₂ ≠ No Critical CHD
Lesions with normal SpO₂ ที่ CAUSE critical illness: coarctation (no cyanosis), severe aortic stenosis (compensated), HLHS with adequate PFO mixing. Pulse ox screening โดยมีขั้นตอน: right hand + foot; ถ้า normal: ยังต้อง examine femoral pulses + four limb BP ทุกราย. Missing femoral pulses → coarctation until echo proves otherwise. False negative rate ~20-30% — clinical exam remains mandatory.

PEARL 4 — HLHS SpO₂ 75-85% is the TARGET, Not a Failure
ในทารก HLHS ก่อนผ่าตัด: SpO₂ 75-85% = appropriate Qp:Qs ~1:1; SpO₂ >90% = too much pulmonary flow = systemic hypoperfusion (worsening metabolic acidosis). อย่าให้ O₂ supplemental ใน HLHS! ↑O₂ → ↓PVR → ↑Qp → ↓Qs → ↑acidosis + ↓BP ยิ่งแย่ขึ้น. ถ้า SpO₂ สูงเกิน + BP ต่ำ + lactate ↑ → ↑PVR (FiO₂ ↓, ↑PEEP, CO₂) ไม่ใช่ให้ O₂ เพิ่ม.

PEARL 5 — Down Syndrome + AVSD: Surgical Timing Critical
Complete AVSD ใน trisomy 21: ผ่าตัดที่ 4-6 เดือน. Non-Down syndrome AVSD: ระวัง ↑risk irreversible PVD เร็วกว่า → early repair มักจำเป็น. Down syndrome: PVR ↓ช้ากว่า → natural protection longer แต่ไม่ใช่เหตุผลผัดผ่อน. ECG ช่วย: superior axis + RVH + Q waves in I, aVL = virtually diagnostic ของ AVSD. ไม่ตอบสนอง O₂ test → PVR สูง → surgical urgency.
10. Evidence Update — Landmark Studies
Mitchell SC et al. Circulation. 1971;43:323: Landmark epidemiologic study establishing CHD prevalence at 1% of live births — foundational data still cited internationally.

Pierpont ME et al. Circulation. 2018;138:e653 (AHA Scientific Statement): Genetic basis for CHD; ~400 associated genes; comprehensive genetic testing algorithm; de novo mutations contribute significantly.
Peterson C et al. JAMA Pediatr. 2014;168:361: Late detection critical CHD — pulse oximetry screening could detect 875 additional cases/year in US; now recommended universally in all newborns.

Bonnet D et al. Circulation. 1999;99:916: Prenatal diagnosis TGA reduces neonatal morbidity and mortality — definitive study supporting fetal echo screening for TGA; preductal SpO₂ at birth nearly normal.
Escobar-Diaz MC et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol. 2015;45:678: Prenatal detection TGA over 20 years: improved but imperfect; outflow views essential for prenatal TGA detection.
Freud LR et al. Circulation. 2014;130:638: Fetal aortic valvuloplasty for evolving HLHS — postnatal outcomes of first 100 patients; prenatal cardiac intervention can alter disease progression.
Jeon B et al. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg. 2020;159:1466: Surgical treatment TOF in symptomatic neonates and young infants — increasing trend toward earlier complete repair; acceptable outcomes.
John MM et al. Ann Thorac Surg. 2019;108:1857: Primary cardiac transplantation for CHD in neonatal period — long-term outcomes; 10-year survival 74% for neonatal transplant.
Zaidi S, Brueckner M. Circ Res. 2017;120:923: Genetics and genomics of CHD — comprehensive review; ~50% of CHD genetic basis unknown; noncoding genome likely important.
Blue GM et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2017;69:859: Advances in genetics of CHD — clinician's guide; timeline from linkage analysis to whole exome sequencing; practical genetic testing algorithm.
Almond CS et al. Circulation. 2010;121:1926: ABO-incompatible heart transplant in infants — comparable outcomes to ABO-compatible; expands donor pool; immune tolerance window in neonates.
West LJ et al. NEJM. 2001;344:793: ABO-incompatible infant heart transplantation — landmark study showing safety and efficacy; immature immune system allows tolerance to foreign ABO antigens.
11. Self-Check Questions พร้อมเฉลย
Q: ทารกแรกเกิด GA 38 wk ดูดีตอนคลอด วันที่ 3 ซีด ไม่ดูดนม SpO₂ 85% femoral pulses impalpable — วินิจฉัยอะไรก่อน?

ตอบ: Ductal-dependent systemic circulation — most likely critical coarctation หรือ HLHS หรือ IAA; ductus arteriosus กำลังปิด → ↓descending aortic flow. Management: เริ่ม PGE₁ 0.05 mcg/kg/min ทันที; ECG + CXR + echo + four-limb BPs (right arm >> left arm + legs → coarctation); impalpable femoral pulses = coarctation/IAA/HLHS until echo proves otherwise; transfer to tertiary center urgently.
Q: D-TGA — pathophysiology ใน parallel circulation และ initial management?

ตอบ: Aorta from RV → desaturated blood recirculates to body; PA from LV → oxygenated blood recirculates to lungs; survival depends on MIXING: PFO + PDA. Management: (1) PGE₁ to maintain DA; (2) lowest effective dose (apnea!); (3) if restrictive PFO + persistent hypoxia + acidosis → BAS within 24-48 hr; (4) arterial switch operation within first week; reversed differential cyanosis (foot SpO₂ > hand) occurs if TGA + PPHN + PDA.
Q: TOF anatomy + 'Tet spell' — mechanism and emergency treatment?

ตอบ: TOF: VSD + overriding aorta + RVOT obstruction + RVH (all from anterior deviation infundibular septum). Tet spell: acute ↑RVOT spasm (from agitation) → ↑RVOT obstruction → ↑R→L shunting via VSD → severe cyanosis → metabolic acidosis → ↑RVOT spasm (vicious cycle). Treatment: (1) Knee-chest position; (2) O₂; (3) Morphine (↓agitation, ↓hyperpnea); (4) IV fluids; (5) Phenylephrine (↑SVR → ↓R→L); (6) Propranolol (↓RVOT spasm). Emergency: GA + IV ketamine; recurrent spells → surgical palliation urgently.
Q: HLHS preoperative management — goal SpO₂ and how to achieve?

ตอบ: Target SpO₂: 75-85% = adequate Qp:Qs ~1:1. Strategies to ↑PVR (↓Qp): (1) Avoid supplemental O₂; (2) FiO₂ 0.17 (N₂ or CO₂ added to circuit — CO₂ more effective); (3) ↑PEEP; (4) permissive hypercapnia. If SpO₂ >90% + ↑lactate + ↓BP → too much Qp → need ↑PVR not ↑O₂. Restrictive PFO: BAS urgently. Milrinone (↓afterload) + vasopressin (systemic support). Monitor lactate + mixed venous sat.
Q: Down syndrome + complete AVSD — clinical findings and surgical timing?

ตอบ: AVSD most common CHD in trisomy 21. ECG: superior axis (-30 to -120°) + RVH + Q waves in I, aVL (virtually diagnostic). Echo: common AV valve + primum ASD + inlet VSD. Assess: ventricular balance + AV valve regurgitation. Surgical repair at 4-6 months; early repair if no ↓PVR (especially non-Down, ↑risk irreversible PVD). During O₂ challenge: Down infants may have PVR variations → caution ↑O₂ (↓PVR → ↑L→R → ↑respiratory distress). CHF management: diuretics + ACE inhibitor.
Q: Obstructed TAPVR — clinical presentation and why is it a surgical emergency?

ตอบ: Obstruction to pulmonary venous return → pulmonary venous HTN → ↑pulmonary capillary pressure → pulmonary edema; profound hypoxemia despite 'full' lungs; NOT improved by O₂. PGE₁ does NOT significantly help (not ductal-dependent mechanism). CXR: normal heart size + 'ground glass/interstitial' pattern (pulmonary edema). Surgical connection of pulmonary venous confluence to LA is ONLY treatment → must be done urgently. Postoperatively: PVR lability + low CO syndrome + risk late pulmonary vein re-stenosis (~20%).
Q: PA/IVS — why assess coronary sinusoids before opening RVOT?

ตอบ: PA/IVS: no antegrade RV output → RV pressure = suprasystemic; sinusoidal channels RV myocardium → coronary arteries → retrograde coronary perfusion. If RVOT opened → RV pressure drops → coronary perfusion drops → myocardial ischemia/infarction. Cardiac catheterization MANDATORY first to define sinusoidal connections. If RV-dependent coronary: RVOT opening CONTRAINDICATED → systemic-PA shunt only → staged single ventricle palliation. If no RV-dependent coronary: RVOT opening ± supplemental shunt.
Q: Ebstein anomaly in neonate — pathophysiology and initial management?

ตอบ: Downward displacement of TV leaflets → 'atrialized' RV + severe TR + RA dilation; functional pulmonary atresia can occur. Management: PGE₁ (maintain DA); O₂ + iNO + mild alkalosis → ↓PVR → RV contributes forward PA flow → wean PGE₁ over days as PVR drops postnatally. Massive cardiomegaly on CXR = hallmark ('wall-to-wall heart'). WPW association → arrhythmia risk. If fails → surgical: TV repair + RV exclusion ± BT shunt; perinatal mortality up to 45% severe cases.
Q: Pulse oximetry screening — what does it detect and what does it miss?

ตอบ: Detects: TGA, PA/IVS, critical PS, HLHS, truncus arteriosus, TAPVR, critical coarctation (differential cyanosis), TOF. Misses: coarctation without cyanosis, large VSD/AVSD (SpO₂ normal), IAA (if DA still open), anomalous coronary. Protocol: right hand + foot after 24 hr; positive if SpO₂ <90% either site, <95% both on 3 measures, or difference >3%. False negative rate ~20-30% → physical exam + femoral pulses still mandatory.
Q: 22q11 deletion — what cardiac lesions and what non-cardiac features to look for?

ตอบ: Cardiac (conotruncal): IAA type B (most specific), truncus arteriosus, TOF, other conotruncal defects; TBX1 gene → disrupted pharyngeal arch development. Non-cardiac: (1) Hypocalcemia (parathyroid absence → check ionized Ca²⁺ in any conotruncal CHD); (2) Thymic aplasia → T-cell deficiency (absent thymic shadow on CXR); (3) Neurodevelopmental delay. Workup: FISH for 22q11 in any conotruncal defect. ALL blood products must be irradiated if 22q11 confirmed (prevent GvHD).
Day 51 Preview

Avery Chapter 51 — Long-Term Neurologic Outcomes in Children With Congenital Heart Disease
พรุ่งนี้: Long-Term Neurologic Outcomes in Children With Congenital Heart Disease (Avery Ch.51) — สัปดาห์ต่อไปเราจะย้ายจาก Cardiovascular System มาสู่ long-term neurological consequences ของ CHD. เนื้อหา: brain injury patterns (IVH, PVL, white matter injury), neurodevelopmental outcomes by CHD type, factors influencing brain development (fetal cerebrovascular effects, intraoperative, postoperative), และ strategies สำหรับ neuroprotection + neurodevelopmental follow-up ของเด็ก CHD.
